questTitle: O-Level Chemistry: Giant Covalent Structures - Allotropes of Carbon Worksheet 1
subject: Chemistry
description: Master giant covalent structures! This worksheet focuses on allotropes of carbon (diamond and graphite), their structures, bonding, properties, and applications. Understand how structure relates to properties including hardness, electrical conductivity, and use as lubricants. Show clear reasoning!

questions.question: What is a giant covalent structure?
options: A structure made up of small molecules held together by weak forces
A structure where millions of atoms are joined together by strong covalent bonds in a continuous network
A structure made up of positive ions surrounded by delocalised electrons
A structure where positive and negative ions are arranged in a lattice
questions.answer: A structure where millions of atoms are joined together by strong covalent bonds in a continuous network

questions.question: Why do giant covalent substances generally have very high melting and boiling points?
options: Because they have weak intermolecular forces that are easy to overcome
Because a large amount of energy is required to break the many strong covalent bonds throughout the structure
Because they contain ionic bonds
Because they have delocalised electrons
questions.answer: Because a large amount of energy is required to break the many strong covalent bonds throughout the structure

questions.question: What are allotropes?
options: Different compounds formed by the same elements
Different physical forms of the same element in the same physical state, with different structural arrangements of atoms
Isotopes of the same element
Different elements with similar properties
questions.answer: Different physical forms of the same element in the same physical state, with different structural arrangements of atoms

questions.question: Diamond and graphite are both allotropes of carbon. What does this mean?
options: They are different compounds containing carbon
They are both made only of carbon atoms but have different structural arrangements
They have the same structure but different properties
They are isotopes of carbon
questions.answer: They are both made only of carbon atoms but have different structural arrangements

questions.question: In diamond, how many other carbon atoms is each carbon atom bonded to?
options: 2 carbon atoms
3 carbon atoms
4 carbon atoms
6 carbon atoms
questions.answer: 4 carbon atoms

questions.question: What type of bonding exists between carbon atoms in diamond?
options: Ionic bonding
Metallic bonding
Strong covalent bonding
Weak intermolecular forces
questions.answer: Strong covalent bonding

questions.question: Describe the overall structure of diamond.
options: Layers of carbon atoms with weak forces between layers
A giant three-dimensional tetrahedral network where each carbon atom is covalently bonded to 4 other carbon atoms
Small molecules of carbon arranged in a lattice
Rings of 6 carbon atoms stacked on top of each other
questions.answer: A giant three-dimensional tetrahedral network where each carbon atom is covalently bonded to 4 other carbon atoms

questions.question: In diamond, how many valence electrons does each carbon atom have, and how are they used?
options: 4 valence electrons; all 4 are used in covalent bonding with neighbouring carbon atoms
4 valence electrons; 3 are used in bonding, 1 is delocalised
6 valence electrons; 4 are used in bonding
4 valence electrons; 2 are used in bonding, 2 are lone pairs
questions.answer: 4 valence electrons; all 4 are used in covalent bonding with neighbouring carbon atoms

questions.question: Why does diamond have no free electrons?
options: Carbon atoms have no valence electrons
All 4 valence electrons of each carbon atom are involved in forming covalent bonds, leaving no free or delocalised electrons
The electrons are transferred to other carbon atoms
Diamond is an ionic compound so has no electrons
questions.answer: All 4 valence electrons of each carbon atom are involved in forming covalent bonds, leaving no free or delocalised electrons

questions.question: Diamond is the hardest known natural substance. Explain why.
options: Diamond has weak intermolecular forces that make it rigid
Each carbon atom is strongly covalently bonded to 4 other carbon atoms in a rigid 3D tetrahedral structure, making it very difficult to break or deform
Diamond contains metallic bonds which are very strong
Diamond has ionic bonds throughout its structure
questions.answer: Each carbon atom is strongly covalently bonded to 4 other carbon atoms in a rigid 3D tetrahedral structure, making it very difficult to break or deform

questions.question: Diamond has a very high melting point (about 3550°C). Why?
options: Diamond has strong intermolecular forces between molecules
A very large amount of energy is required to break the many strong covalent bonds throughout the giant covalent structure
Diamond contains ionic bonds which require lots of energy to break
Diamond has delocalised electrons that hold the structure together
questions.answer: A very large amount of energy is required to break the many strong covalent bonds throughout the giant covalent structure

questions.question: Does diamond conduct electricity? Explain.
options: Yes, because it contains carbon atoms which have electrons
Yes, because it has delocalised electrons that can move
No, because all valence electrons are used in covalent bonding and there are no free/delocalised electrons or ions to carry charge
No, because diamond is a gas at room temperature
questions.answer: No, because all valence electrons are used in covalent bonding and there are no free/delocalised electrons or ions to carry charge

questions.question: Diamond is used in cutting tools and drill bits. Which property makes it suitable for this use?
options: Its ability to conduct electricity
Its extreme hardness due to its strong 3D covalent network structure
Its low melting point
Its layered structure
questions.answer: Its extreme hardness due to its strong 3D covalent network structure

questions.question: A student claims: 'Diamond is hard because carbon-carbon bonds are the strongest bonds.' Is this reasoning complete?
options: Yes, this is the only reason
No. While C-C bonds are strong, diamond's hardness is also due to each carbon forming 4 bonds in a rigid 3D tetrahedral arrangement with no weak points or layers
No, diamond is hard because it has ionic bonds
No, diamond is hard because of delocalised electrons
questions.answer: No. While C-C bonds are strong, diamond's hardness is also due to each carbon forming 4 bonds in a rigid 3D tetrahedral arrangement with no weak points or layers

questions.question: In graphite, how many other carbon atoms is each carbon atom bonded to?
options: 2 carbon atoms
3 carbon atoms
4 carbon atoms
6 carbon atoms
questions.answer: 3 carbon atoms

questions.question: Describe the arrangement of carbon atoms within each layer of graphite.
options: Carbon atoms are arranged randomly
Carbon atoms are arranged in a tetrahedral structure
Carbon atoms are arranged in flat hexagonal rings (honeycomb pattern)
Carbon atoms are arranged in square rings
questions.answer: Carbon atoms are arranged in flat hexagonal rings (honeycomb pattern)

questions.question: What type of bonding exists between carbon atoms within each layer of graphite?
options: Weak intermolecular forces
Ionic bonding
Strong covalent bonding
Metallic bonding
questions.answer: Strong covalent bonding

questions.question: What holds the layers of graphite together?
options: Strong covalent bonds between layers
Weak intermolecular forces of attraction between layers
Ionic bonds between layers
Metallic bonds between layers
questions.answer: Weak intermolecular forces of attraction between layers

questions.question: Each carbon atom in graphite has 4 valence electrons but only forms 3 covalent bonds. What happens to the fourth electron?
options: It is lost to form a positive ion
It remains as a lone pair on the carbon atom
It becomes delocalised and can move freely along the layers
It forms an ionic bond with another carbon atom
questions.answer: It becomes delocalised and can move freely along the layers

questions.question: Compare the bonding within layers and between layers in graphite.
options: Strong covalent bonds within layers; strong covalent bonds between layers
Weak intermolecular forces within layers; strong covalent bonds between layers
Strong covalent bonds within layers (each C bonded to 3 others); weak intermolecular forces of attraction between layers
Ionic bonds within layers; covalent bonds between layers
questions.answer: Strong covalent bonds within layers (each C bonded to 3 others); weak intermolecular forces of attraction between layers

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 4: Properties of Graphite - Melting Point
questions.question: Graphite has a very high melting point (about 3600°C). Why?
options: Because the layers are held together by strong forces
Because a large amount of energy is needed to break the strong covalent bonds between carbon atoms within the layers
Because graphite contains ionic bonds
Because graphite has weak intermolecular forces
questions.answer: Because a large amount of energy is needed to break the strong covalent bonds between carbon atoms within the layers

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 4: Properties of Graphite - Softness and Lubrication
questions.question: Why is graphite soft and slippery?
options: Because the covalent bonds within layers are weak
Because the layers are held together by weak intermolecular forces, allowing layers to slide over each other easily
Because graphite has a low melting point
Because graphite contains delocalised electrons that make it slippery
questions.answer: Because the layers are held together by weak intermolecular forces, allowing layers to slide over each other easily

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 4: Properties of Graphite - Softness and Lubrication
questions.question: Graphite is used as a lubricant in machinery. Which structural feature makes it suitable for this use?
options: Its strong covalent bonds within layers
Its layered structure with weak intermolecular forces between layers, allowing layers to slide over each other easily
Its delocalised electrons
Its tetrahedral structure
questions.answer: Its layered structure with weak intermolecular forces between layers, allowing layers to slide over each other easily

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 4: Properties of Graphite - Electrical Conductivity
questions.question: Can graphite conduct electricity? Explain.
options: No, because graphite is a non-metal
No, because graphite has no charged particles
Yes, because each carbon atom has one delocalised electron that is free to move along the layers and carry charge
Yes, because graphite contains ions
questions.answer: Yes, because each carbon atom has one delocalised electron that is free to move along the layers and carry charge

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 4: Properties of Graphite - Electrical Conductivity
questions.question: Graphite is used in electrodes. Why is it suitable for this purpose?
options: Because it has a low melting point
Because it can conduct electricity due to delocalised electrons, and has a high melting point so won't melt during use
Because it is very hard
Because it is an insulator
questions.answer: Because it can conduct electricity due to delocalised electrons, and has a high melting point so won't melt during use

questions.question: Graphite is used in pencil 'leads'. Which property is most relevant?
options: Its high electrical conductivity
Its softness and ability to leave marks as layers slide off onto paper
Its high melting point
Its hardness
questions.answer: Its softness and ability to leave marks as layers slide off onto paper

questions.question: A student states: 'Graphite can conduct electricity because it contains carbon atoms.' Is this explanation correct?
options: Yes, all carbon-containing substances conduct electricity
No. Graphite conducts because each carbon is bonded to only 3 others, leaving one delocalised electron per carbon atom that is free to move and carry charge
No, graphite cannot conduct electricity
Yes, carbon atoms contain protons which carry charge
questions.answer: No. Graphite conducts because each carbon is bonded to only 3 others, leaving one delocalised electron per carbon atom that is free to move and carry charge

questions.question: Both diamond and graphite are made entirely of carbon atoms. Why do they have such different properties?
options: They contain different isotopes of carbon
They have different structural arrangements of carbon atoms
Diamond contains impurities while graphite is pure
They have different types of carbon atoms
questions.answer: They have different structural arrangements of carbon atoms

questions.question: Compare the number of covalent bonds each carbon atom forms in diamond and graphite.
options: Diamond: 3 bonds; Graphite: 4 bonds
Diamond: 4 bonds; Graphite: 3 bonds
Both: 4 bonds
Both: 3 bonds
questions.answer: Diamond: 4 bonds; Graphite: 3 bonds

questions.question: Compare the hardness of diamond and graphite, and explain the difference.
options: Both are equally hard because both have strong covalent bonds
Diamond is very hard (rigid 3D structure with no weak points); graphite is soft (layers can slide over each other due to weak intermolecular forces between layers)
Graphite is harder because it has delocalised electrons
Diamond is soft; graphite is hard
questions.answer: Diamond is very hard (rigid 3D structure with no weak points); graphite is soft (layers can slide over each other due to weak intermolecular forces between layers)

questions.question: Compare the electrical conductivity of diamond and graphite.
options: Both conduct electricity due to delocalised electrons
Neither conducts electricity
Diamond does not conduct (no free electrons); graphite conducts (has delocalised electrons)
Diamond conducts; graphite does not conduct
questions.answer: Diamond does not conduct (no free electrons); graphite conducts (has delocalised electrons)

questions.question: Both diamond and graphite have very high melting points. Why is this, despite their other different properties?
options: Both have ionic bonds that need lots of energy to break
Both have strong covalent bonds between carbon atoms within their structures that require a large amount of energy to break
Both have metallic bonding
Both have weak intermolecular forces
questions.answer: Both have strong covalent bonds between carbon atoms within their structures that require a large amount of energy to break

questions.question:
Complete the comparison table:

| Property | Diamond | Graphite |
|----------|---------|----------|
| Bonds per C atom | ? | ? |
| Delocalised electrons | ? | ? |
| Hardness | ? | ? |
| Electrical conductivity | ? | ? |
options: Diamond: 4, Yes, Soft, Conducts; Graphite: 3, No, Hard, Does not conduct
Diamond: 4, No, Hard, Does not conduct; Graphite: 3, Yes, Soft, Conducts
Diamond: 3, Yes, Soft, Conducts; Graphite: 4, No, Hard, Does not conduct
Diamond: 4, No, Soft, Conducts; Graphite: 3, Yes, Hard, Does not conduct
questions.answer: Diamond: 4, No, Hard, Does not conduct; Graphite: 3, Yes, Soft, Conducts

questions.question: Boron nitride (BN) exists in a form similar to graphite, with layers of hexagonal rings. Based on this structure, predict whether this form of boron nitride would be a good lubricant.
options: No, because it contains nitrogen
Yes, because like graphite, it has a layered structure with weak intermolecular forces between layers, allowing layers to slide over each other
No, because it would be too hard
Yes, because it can conduct electricity
questions.answer: Yes, because like graphite, it has a layered structure with weak intermolecular forces between layers, allowing layers to slide over each other

questions.question: Silicon carbide (SiC) has a structure similar to diamond, where each silicon atom is covalently bonded to 4 carbon atoms in a tetrahedral arrangement. Predict its hardness.
options: Very soft, because it contains silicon
Very hard, because like diamond, it has a rigid 3D network of strong covalent bonds
Moderately hard, because carbon is harder than silicon
Soft, because the layers can slide
questions.answer: Very hard, because like diamond, it has a rigid 3D network of strong covalent bonds

questions.question:
A substance has the following properties:
- Very high melting point
- Layered structure with hexagonal rings
- Layers can slide over each other
- Conducts electricity

Which structure is it most similar to?
options: Diamond
Graphite
Sodium chloride
Simple covalent molecule
questions.answer: Graphite

questions.question: A substance has a structure where each atom is bonded to 4 other atoms in a tetrahedral arrangement, with all valence electrons used in bonding. Predict its electrical conductivity.
options: Good conductor, because it has many electrons
Does not conduct, because all valence electrons are used in bonding with no delocalised electrons
Conducts when molten only
Conducts when dissolved in water
questions.answer: Does not conduct, because all valence electrons are used in bonding with no delocalised electrons

questions.question: Molybdenum disulfide (MoS₂) has a layered structure similar to graphite, with strong bonds within layers and weak forces between layers. Predict its properties and uses.
options: Hard and used in cutting tools
Soft and slippery; can be used as a lubricant because layers can slide over each other due to weak intermolecular forces between layers
Low melting point; used as a fuel
Soluble in water; used as a cleaning agent
questions.answer: Soft and slippery; can be used as a lubricant because layers can slide over each other due to weak intermolecular forces between layers

questions.question: Diamond is used in jewelry while graphite is used in pencils. Explain why they are suitable for these different uses.
options: Diamond is shiny and very hard (doesn't scratch easily); graphite is soft and leaves marks as layers slide off
Both are equally suitable for either use
Diamond is cheap; graphite is expensive
Diamond is soft; graphite is hard
questions.answer: Diamond is shiny and very hard (doesn't scratch easily); graphite is soft and leaves marks as layers slide off

questions.question: Graphite is used as electrodes in electrolysis while diamond is not. Explain why.
options: Diamond is too expensive
Graphite can conduct electricity (delocalised electrons) while diamond cannot (no free electrons)
Diamond has a lower melting point
Graphite is harder than diamond
questions.answer: Graphite can conduct electricity (delocalised electrons) while diamond cannot (no free electrons)

questions.question: Both diamond and graphite remain solid at very high temperatures. Explain why both have very high melting points.
options: Both contain weak intermolecular forces
Both have giant covalent structures with many strong covalent bonds that require a large amount of energy to break
Both contain ionic bonds
Both contain metallic bonds
questions.answer: Both have giant covalent structures with many strong covalent bonds that require a large amount of energy to break

questions.question: A student rubs a graphite pencil on paper and observes that grey marks are left behind. Another student tries to scratch diamond with steel and observes no marks on the diamond. Explain both observations in terms of structure.
options: Graphite is wet; diamond is dry
Graphite has weak intermolecular forces between layers allowing layers to slide off onto paper; diamond has a rigid 3D structure with no weak points, making it harder than steel
Both are due to the colour of the substances
Graphite has more carbon atoms than diamond
questions.answer: Graphite has weak intermolecular forces between layers allowing layers to slide off onto paper; diamond has a rigid 3D structure with no weak points, making it harder than steel

questions.question: A student states: 'Graphite is soft because it has weak covalent bonds between carbon atoms.' Is this correct?
options: Yes, graphite has weak covalent bonds
No. The covalent bonds within layers are strong. Graphite is soft because the layers are held together by weak intermolecular forces, allowing them to slide over each other
Yes, all bonds in graphite are weak
No, graphite is actually very hard
questions.answer: No. The covalent bonds within layers are strong. Graphite is soft because the layers are held together by weak intermolecular forces, allowing them to slide over each other

questions.question: A student states: 'Diamond cannot conduct electricity because it is a non-metal.' Is this explanation complete?
options: Yes, non-metals never conduct electricity

No. The correct explanation is that all 4 valence electrons of each carbon are used in covalent bonding, leaving no free/delocalised electrons to carry charge. (Graphite is also a non-metal but conducts)
Yes, only metals conduct electricity
No, diamond can conduct electricity
questions.answer: No. The correct explanation is that all 4 valence electrons of each carbon are used in covalent bonding, leaving no free/delocalised electrons to carry charge. (Graphite is also a non-metal but conducts)

questions.question: A student states: 'Diamond has a higher melting point than graphite because diamond has 4 bonds per carbon while graphite only has 3.' Is this reasoning valid?
options: Yes, more bonds always means higher melting point
No. Both have very similar high melting points because both have strong covalent bonds. The number of bonds per atom is not the only factor
Yes, this is the correct explanation
No, graphite has a higher melting point than diamond
questions.answer: No. Both have very similar high melting points because both have strong covalent bonds. The number of bonds per atom is not the only factor

questions.question: Explain why graphite can conduct electricity but diamond cannot, even though both are made of carbon atoms.
options: Graphite contains different carbon atoms than diamond

In graphite, each carbon forms 3 bonds, leaving 1 delocalised electron per carbon that is free to move and carry charge. In diamond, each carbon forms 4 bonds, using all valence electrons, so there are no free electrons
Diamond is harder so electrons cannot move
Graphite contains impurities that conduct electricity
questions.answer: In graphite, each carbon forms 3 bonds, leaving 1 delocalised electron per carbon that is free to move and carry charge. In diamond, each carbon forms 4 bonds, using all valence electrons, so there are no free electrons

questions.question: Explain why graphite is used as a lubricant while diamond is used in cutting tools.
options: Diamond is cheaper than graphite

Graphite has a layered structure with weak intermolecular forces between layers (layers slide easily = good lubricant); diamond has a rigid 3D structure with strong bonds in all directions (extremely hard = good for cutting)
Both are actually used for the same purposes
Graphite is harder than diamond
questions.answer: Graphite has a layered structure with weak intermolecular forces between layers (layers slide easily = good lubricant); diamond has a rigid 3D structure with strong bonds in all directions (extremely hard = good for cutting)

questions.question:
A new material is discovered with the following structure:
- Each atom is bonded to 3 other atoms in hexagonal layers
- Layers are held together by weak intermolecular forces
- One electron per atom is delocalised

Predict THREE properties of this material.
options: Hard, does not conduct, low melting point
Soft/slippery (layers can slide), conducts electricity (delocalised electrons), high melting point (strong covalent bonds within layers)
Hard, conducts electricity, low melting point
Soft, does not conduct, low melting point
questions.answer: Soft/slippery (layers can slide), conducts electricity (delocalised electrons), high melting point (strong covalent bonds within layers)

questions.question: Compare giant covalent structures (like diamond) with simple covalent structures (like carbon dioxide, CO₂) in terms of melting point and structure.
options: Both have low melting points due to weak bonds
Both have high melting points due to strong covalent bonds
Giant covalent: high MP (must break many strong covalent bonds); Simple covalent: low MP (only weak intermolecular forces between molecules need to be overcome)
Giant covalent: low MP; Simple covalent: high MP
questions.answer: Giant covalent: high MP (must break many strong covalent bonds); Simple covalent: low MP (only weak intermolecular forces between molecules need to be overcome)

questions.question:
A substance X has the following properties:
- Melting point: 3550°C
- Does not conduct electricity
- Extremely hard
- Made of only one type of atom, each bonded to 4 others

Identify substance X and explain how its structure leads to these properties.
options: Graphite; layered structure with delocalised electrons

Diamond; giant covalent structure where each carbon is bonded to 4 others in a rigid 3D network. High MP due to strong covalent bonds. Hard due to rigid structure with no weak points. Does not conduct as all electrons used in bonding
Sodium chloride; ionic structure with strong electrostatic forces
Silicon dioxide; ionic structure
questions.answer: Diamond; giant covalent structure where each carbon is bonded to 4 others in a rigid 3D network. High MP due to strong covalent bonds. Hard due to rigid structure with no weak points. Does not conduct as all electrons used in bonding
